front: What is the definition of Software Quality?
back: Software Quality is the degree to which a software product meets stated and implied needs when used under specified conditions. It is a combination of functional quality (how well it meets requirements) and non-functional quality (how well it performs, its security, usability, etc.).

front: What is the difference between Verification and Validation?
back: Verification is the process of evaluating whether a product, service, or system complies with a regulation, requirement, specification, or imposed condition ('Are we building the product right?'). Validation is the process of ensuring a system meets the needs and requirements of the customer and other stakeholders ('Are we building the right product?').

front: What is the purpose of Software Quality Assurance (SQA)?
back: Software Quality Assurance is a set of activities that define and assess the adequacy of software processes to provide evidence that establishes confidence that the software processes are appropriate for and produce software products of suitable quality for their intended purposes. It focuses on process improvement and defect prevention.

front: What are the three main types of software quality attributes as defined in the ISO 25010 standard?
back: The ISO/IEC 25010 standard categorizes quality attributes into: 1) Functional Suitability (completeness, correctness, appropriateness), 2) Quality in Use (effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction, freedom from risk, context coverage), and 3) Product Quality (performance efficiency, compatibility, usability, reliability, security, maintainability, portability).

front: What is the Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI) and how does it relate to software quality?
back: CMMI is a process level improvement training and appraisal program. It provides a framework for improving processes in an organization, which directly leads to higher quality software. The model has five maturity levels (Initial, Managed, Defined, Quantitatively Managed, Optimizing) that represent a path from ad-hoc processes to a culture of continuous improvement and defect prevention.
